task.description: WTI Crude Oil continuous front-month futures Close price (yfinance symbol: CL=F), projected 5, 10, and 21 trading days ahead.

spec.description: Prospective/out-of-sample evaluation period in 2026 for daily WTI crude oil. Evaluates selected contender models on 8 weekly origins during the early 2026 geopolitical price shock to measure adaptive real-time forecasting performance.

metadata.agent_rationale: The WTI volatility regime as of February 2, 2026 is classified as normal (32.4% annualized, within the 20-35% threshold), with no anomalous daily moves (z-score of last move is -0.17). Under these conditions, the AutoARIMA baseline forecasts serve as our calibrated anchor. News context reveals immediate price support from extended OPEC+ cuts through March 2026 and elevated geopolitical tension (Persian Gulf and Venezuela). Over the longer term, structural market surpluses and non-OPEC+ supply expansion are expected to cap the upside, aligning well with the baseline's slight mean-reversion at 21 business days. All 11 standard quantiles are generated using an asymmetric normal distribution centered around the point forecasts, preserving the baseline's point estimates and the 80% confidence boundaries.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 5-day horizon, prices are strongly supported near current levels ($65.21) by the recent bullish momentum and key near-term catalysts. OPEC+ has extended production freezes through March 2026, which restricts immediate supply. Geopolitical risk in the Persian Gulf and Venezuela, combined with tightening US commercial crude inventories (3% below the five-year average), keeps a firm floor under prompt prices. This matches the AutoARIMA point forecast of 65.32. The volatility regime is classified as normal (32.4% annualized), resulting in a standard, un-widened 80% prediction interval of [60.25, 70.54].

metadata.agent_rationale: The WTI volatility regime as of February 2, 2026 is classified as normal (32.4% annualized, within the 20-35% threshold), with no anomalous daily moves (z-score of last move is -0.17). Under these conditions, the AutoARIMA baseline forecasts serve as our calibrated anchor. News context reveals immediate price support from extended OPEC+ cuts through March 2026 and elevated geopolitical tension (Persian Gulf and Venezuela). Over the longer term, structural market surpluses and non-OPEC+ supply expansion are expected to cap the upside, aligning well with the baseline's slight mean-reversion at 21 business days. All 11 standard quantiles are generated using an asymmetric normal distribution centered around the point forecasts, preserving the baseline's point estimates and the 80% confidence boundaries.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 21-day horizon, prices are expected to feel moderate downward pressure, mean-reverting toward $63.97. While OPEC+ freezes remain active, long-term concerns regarding a persistent global surplus dominate. Forecasters (like the IEA) anticipate non-OPEC+ supply growth (led by the US, Brazil, and Guyana) to outpace modest demand growth (estimated at 930k bpd for 2026). This limits the long-term price upside, making the downward-sloping AutoARIMA baseline of 63.97 highly plausible. The normal volatility regime translates to an 80% confidence interval of [54.32, 75.20].

metadata.agent_rationale: Anchored to the AutoARIMA baseline forecasts to avoid the extrapolation bias of short-term linear trend projections under elevated volatility conditions (annualised vol classified at 36.4%). The point forecasts and standard quantiles are systematically constructed using a split-normal calibration of the baseline's quantiles, reflecting appropriate uncertainty ranges under current OPEC+ supply cuts and global macroeconomic conditions.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 10-day horizon, supply schedules play a key role. The OPEC+ 8 core group recently extended their freeze on gradual production returns through February and March 2026, providing a solid floor. To prevent extrapolation error (which would project $68.76 via a linear model), we anchor our point forecast on the AutoARIMA baseline of $62.99. This represents a flat, stable trend which has been proven to have much lower MAE in backtesting. The 80% interval of [$55.66, $70.99] is generated using split-normal interpolation to align with the elevated volatility regime.

metadata.agent_rationale: Anchored to the AutoARIMA baseline forecasts to avoid the extrapolation bias of short-term linear trend projections under elevated volatility conditions (annualised vol classified at 36.4%). The point forecasts and standard quantiles are systematically constructed using a split-normal calibration of the baseline's quantiles, reflecting appropriate uncertainty ranges under current OPEC+ supply cuts and global macroeconomic conditions.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 21-day horizon, statistical signals lose predictive power and are highly sensitive to trend extrapolation bias (which would model $72.96). Macro demand indicators and geopolitical risk are dominant instead: IEA reports stronger non-OECD demand growth, Middle East geopolitical premium remains elevated, and US SPR refilling continues. We anchor our forecast to the AutoARIMA baseline point forecast of $63.51. The split-normal quantiles establish a wide 80% interval of [$53.77, $75.46], which captures the elevated uncertainty and aligns with flat-trend outperformance observed in prior elevated volatility periods.

metadata.agent_rationale: Our forecasting strategy integrates a statistical baseline with short-term trend projection and current news context. Volatility regime analysis classifies WTI crude oil as being in an 'elevated' regime (rolling annualized volatility at 35.8%, which falls in the 35%-55% range), and no significant daily price anomaly is flagged (z-score = +0.04). In accordance with our strategy, we widen the AutoARIMA baseline's prediction intervals by 12.5% across all horizons to prevent undercoverage under elevated volatility. Short-term point forecasts are adjusted slightly upward from their baseline anchors to incorporate positive momentum from OPEC+'s Feb 1 voluntary cut extensions and geopolitical friction in the Gulf, while the long-term forecast is anchored back to the baseline in response to bearish global macro demand growth projections for the second half of 2026.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the short-term 5-day horizon (corresponding to calendar Feb 20, 2026), momentum and near-term catalysts support WTI prices slightly above the current $62.89 close. OPEC+'s Feb 1 decision to extend its voluntary cuts of 1.65 mb/d through March 2026 defends price floors, while elevated geopolitical tension near the Strait of Hormuz acts as a positive price driver. We adjust the AutoARIMA baseline of 63.14 upward to 63.50, blending in the short-term linear trend projection (64.39). The rolling annualized volatility is classified as elevated at 35.8%, so we widen the prediction intervals by 12.5% from the statistical baseline to capture current market noise.

metadata.agent_rationale: Our forecasting strategy integrates a statistical baseline with short-term trend projection and current news context. Volatility regime analysis classifies WTI crude oil as being in an 'elevated' regime (rolling annualized volatility at 35.8%, which falls in the 35%-55% range), and no significant daily price anomaly is flagged (z-score = +0.04). In accordance with our strategy, we widen the AutoARIMA baseline's prediction intervals by 12.5% across all horizons to prevent undercoverage under elevated volatility. Short-term point forecasts are adjusted slightly upward from their baseline anchors to incorporate positive momentum from OPEC+'s Feb 1 voluntary cut extensions and geopolitical friction in the Gulf, while the long-term forecast is anchored back to the baseline in response to bearish global macro demand growth projections for the second half of 2026.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 10-day horizon (corresponding to calendar Feb 27, 2026), prices are projected to remain relatively stable around $63.20. We adjust the baseline forecast of 62.79 slightly upward to incorporate the active geopolitical risk premium and OPEC+ compliance, while accounting for a potential minor softening as temporary North American winter weather freeze-offs fully resolve. The standard deviations are widened by 12.5% in response to the elevated volatility regime (35.8% rolling vol), ensuring appropriate probabilistic coverage.

metadata.agent_rationale: Our forecasting strategy integrates a statistical baseline with short-term trend projection and current news context. Volatility regime analysis classifies WTI crude oil as being in an 'elevated' regime (rolling annualized volatility at 35.8%, which falls in the 35%-55% range), and no significant daily price anomaly is flagged (z-score = +0.04). In accordance with our strategy, we widen the AutoARIMA baseline's prediction intervals by 12.5% across all horizons to prevent undercoverage under elevated volatility. Short-term point forecasts are adjusted slightly upward from their baseline anchors to incorporate positive momentum from OPEC+'s Feb 1 voluntary cut extensions and geopolitical friction in the Gulf, while the long-term forecast is anchored back to the baseline in response to bearish global macro demand growth projections for the second half of 2026.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the long-term 21-day horizon (corresponding to calendar Mar 16, 2026), macroeconomic demand fundamentals and published analyst consensus act as a strong cap on prices, resisting the short-term linear extrapolation of $65.54. Reports from the IEA and EIA forecast a bearish balance of oversupply and inventory builds in 2026 (EIA Brent forecast of $58/bbl translates to ~$52-$54 for WTI). Therefore, we closely anchor the point forecast to the baseline of 63.44 (at 63.50), reflecting a balanced view where persistent geopolitical premiums in the Gulf are neutralized by weak macro demand. Standard deviations are widened by 12.5% for the elevated volatility regime.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI crude oil pricing as of Feb 23, 2026, is shaped by a transition to an elevated volatility regime (37.23% annualized rolling vol) driven by acute geopolitical tensions in the Strait of Hormuz and OPEC+'s extended production pause through March. We anchor to the flat-trend ARIMA baseline points ($66.60, $66.23, $66.61) as a robust alternative to a linear trend projection model, which is prone to trend-reversal errors when recent spike momentum is drag-weighted by older, lower-priced history. Prediction intervals are reconstructed and smoothed using a split-normal distribution around the baseline's 10% and 90% quantiles to properly encapsulate the elevated risk environment.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the short-term horizon (5 business days), near-term momentum and geopolitical risk premium dominate. WTI crude oil prices have surged from $62.89 on Feb 13 to $66.39 on Feb 20, driven by acute geopolitical tensions in the Strait of Hormuz (following the Stena Imperative incident on Feb 3 and President Trump's public warnings on Feb 20 of a potential limited attack on Iran). We anchor to the ARIMA baseline point of $66.60. Due to the elevated volatility regime (annualized rolling standard deviation of 37.23%), we calibrate wide intervals using a split-normal distribution around the baseline points to reflect the high-tension maritime risk.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI crude oil pricing as of Feb 23, 2026, is shaped by a transition to an elevated volatility regime (37.23% annualized rolling vol) driven by acute geopolitical tensions in the Strait of Hormuz and OPEC+'s extended production pause through March. We anchor to the flat-trend ARIMA baseline points ($66.60, $66.23, $66.61) as a robust alternative to a linear trend projection model, which is prone to trend-reversal errors when recent spike momentum is drag-weighted by older, lower-priced history. Prediction intervals are reconstructed and smoothed using a split-normal distribution around the baseline's 10% and 90% quantiles to properly encapsulate the elevated risk environment.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the medium-term horizon (10 business days), the supply outlook remains supported by OPEC+'s Feb 1, 2026 decision to extend voluntary production adjustments (including 2.2 mb/d and 1.65 mb/d cuts) through March 2026. This extension establishes a firm floor around the mid-$60s for WTI crude oil. Given the elevated volatility, we anchor to the flat-trend ARIMA point of $66.23 and apply split-normal calibration to the baseline's 10% and 90% quantiles, preserving the wider uncertainty envelope required in this high-risk regime.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI crude oil pricing as of Feb 23, 2026, is shaped by a transition to an elevated volatility regime (37.23% annualized rolling vol) driven by acute geopolitical tensions in the Strait of Hormuz and OPEC+'s extended production pause through March. We anchor to the flat-trend ARIMA baseline points ($66.60, $66.23, $66.61) as a robust alternative to a linear trend projection model, which is prone to trend-reversal errors when recent spike momentum is drag-weighted by older, lower-priced history. Prediction intervals are reconstructed and smoothed using a split-normal distribution around the baseline's 10% and 90% quantiles to properly encapsulate the elevated risk environment.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the long-term horizon (21 business days), macroeconomic supply/demand dynamics and persistent geopolitical risk premium compete. While global investment consensus (e.g., J.P. Morgan and S&P Global) forecasts Brent averages near $60-$63/bbl for Q1 2026 due to an anticipated global supply surplus of ~1 mb/d, WTI spot prices remain supported by geopolitical risk in the Middle East and a massive 9-million-barrel drop in U.S. commercial inventories reported in mid-February. The resulting balance anchors to the flat-trend ARIMA point of $66.61, with wide-tailed uncertainty intervals reflecting potential outcomes from sudden de-escalation to severe supply disruptions.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI crude oil is undergoing a massive structural break as of the March 2, 2026 forecast origin. The outbreak of a conflict involving the U.S., Israel, and Iran on February 28 led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, blocking 20% of global oil traffic. Consequently, WTI has jumped from a Friday close of $67.02 to a Monday close of $71.23, rendering the pre-escalation AutoARIMA baseline obsolete. To capture this profound change, we have classified the volatility regime as elevated (rolling vol of 35.5% and expected to surge) and applied an upward calibration to the point forecasts using a log-normal distribution with 50% annualised volatility. This approach integrates the explosive positive skew of a physical chokepoint shutdown with appropriate statistical discipline.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 5-day horizon, the immediate momentum from the massive geopolitical escalation on February 28, 2026 (the closure of the Strait of Hormuz) dominates. The market closed at $71.23 on March 2, testing a high of $75.33. With tanker traffic down 70% and sea mines reported in the chokepoint, the statistical ARIMA baseline of $66.94 is severely out of date. We calibrate the point forecast upward to $76.00, reflecting immediate risk premium and upward momentum. Quantiles are modeled via a log-normal distribution with an elevated annualised volatility of 50% to capture the positive skew of this supply shock.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI crude oil is undergoing a massive structural break as of the March 2, 2026 forecast origin. The outbreak of a conflict involving the U.S., Israel, and Iran on February 28 led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, blocking 20% of global oil traffic. To capture this profound change, we have classified the volatility regime as elevated (rolling vol of 35.5% and expected to surge) and applied an upward calibration to the point forecasts using a log-normal distribution with 50% annualised volatility. This approach integrates the explosive positive skew of a physical chokepoint shutdown with appropriate statistical discipline.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 10-day horizon, physical oil shortages will begin to manifest globally if the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz continues. OPEC+ maintained its production cuts at the February meeting, leaving the market highly vulnerable to this disruption of 20% of global oil trade. We calibrate the point forecast to $81.00. Sizing the 80% prediction interval with 50% annualised volatility reflects the widening range of potential outcomes, with the upper tail (95th percentile at $95.42) capturing severe supply squeeze scenarios.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI crude oil is undergoing a massive structural break as of the March 2, 2026 forecast origin. The outbreak of a conflict involving the U.S., Israel, and Iran on February 28 led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, blocking 20% of global oil traffic. Consequently, WTI has jumped from a Friday close of $67.02 to a Monday close of $71.23, rendering the pre-escalation AutoARIMA baseline obsolete. To capture this profound change, we have classified the volatility regime as elevated (rolling vol of 35.5% and expected to surge) and applied an upward calibration to the point forecasts using a log-normal distribution with 50% annualised volatility. This approach integrates the explosive positive skew of a physical chokepoint shutdown with appropriate statistical discipline.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 21-day horizon, statistical signals have completely broken down, and pricing is driven entirely by geopolitical and supply-security risks. If the Hormuz blockade persists for three weeks, global inventories will be depleted, forcing prices towards $100/bbl. We calibrate the point forecast to $88.00. The log-normal distribution beautifully models the asymmetric skew of this event: the downside is heavily protected around the $69-$73 range by high demand and supply shortages, while the upside tail extends past $111, capturing a potentially historic price spike.

metadata.agent_rationale: We classify the current volatility regime as 'elevated' with a rolling annualized volatility of 52.5% and a major daily move anomaly on March 6 (z = +3.94, +$9.89 daily close change). This triggers an adaptive trend window of 15 days, showing a massive short-term upward trend (+1.44 USD/day). Grounded web search shows that U.S.-Israeli airstrikes on Iran (started Feb 28) and Iran's subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz (March 7) have created an unprecedented physical supply deficit. Since WTI has already risen to $94.77 on March 9 and Brent has crossed $100/bbl, the AutoARIMA baseline's mean-reverting/flat-trend projections around $91 are too conservative. We adjust the point forecasts upwards to $97.00, $98.50, and $108.00, and apply a 12.5% widening to the 80% prediction intervals.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 5 business day horizon, strong upward momentum and physical supply panic dominate the market. Following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz on March 7 and Brent crude surging past $100/bbl on March 8, WTI has already closed at $94.77 on March 9. The linear trend projection from a shortened 15-day adaptive window (+1.44 USD/day) is trusted and supplemented by the strong near-term geopolitical catalyst, pushing the point forecast to $97.00. Intervals are widened by 12.5% to reflect the elevated vol regime.

metadata.agent_rationale: We classify the current volatility regime as 'elevated' with a rolling annualized volatility of 52.5% and a major daily move anomaly on March 6 (z = +3.94, +$9.89 daily close change). This triggers an adaptive trend window of 15 days, showing a massive short-term upward trend (+1.44 USD/day). Grounded web search shows that U.S.-Israeli airstrikes on Iran (started Feb 28) and Iran's subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz (March 7) have created an unprecedented physical supply deficit. We adjust the point forecasts upwards to $97.00, $98.50, and $108.00, and apply a 12.5% widening to the 80% prediction intervals.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 10 business day horizon, geopolitical risk premium remains extremely severe as military conflict in the Persian Gulf persists. The OPEC+ agreement on March 1 to gradually unwind voluntary cuts by 206,000 bpd starting in April is completely overshadowed by the 7-10 mbd supply disruption caused by the blockaded Strait of Hormuz. WTI is projected to trade in the upper $90s, anchored to $98.50, with prediction intervals widened by 12.5% to reflect high uncertainty.

metadata.agent_rationale: We classify the current volatility regime as 'elevated' with a rolling annualized volatility of 52.5% and a major daily move anomaly on March 6 (z = +3.94, +$9.89 daily close change). metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 21 business day horizon, structural supply scarcity and geopolitical disruption fully dominate the market over statistical signals. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz traps over 20% of global oil transit, and the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) is at a depleted historical low of 409 million barrels, restricting major emergency interventions. With Brent in triple digits and analysts rapidly revising price targets, WTI is projected to break into triple digits, anchored to $108.00. Intervals are widened by 12.5%.

metadata.agent_rationale: This probabilistic forecast integrates the statistical ARIMA baseline with crucial market context as of March 16, 2026. The market is in an extreme volatility regime (rolling annualized vol of 70.8%) due to Middle East hostilities and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Although short-term momentum is strongly bullish, a record coordinated 400-million-barrel emergency stock release by the IEA and US SPR (starting the week of March 16) acts as a near-term ceiling. Point forecasts are anchored to the ARIMA baseline (which matches the flat-trend strategy that avoids over-extrapolation in high-vol regimes). Prediction intervals are widened by 12.5% to reflect extreme volatility and are modeled via a log-normal distribution to preserve mild right-skewness.
metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 5-day horizon, WTI crude is forecasted to remain near the psychological $98.81 level, anchored to the statistical ARIMA baseline. While recent trend extrapolation suggests a rapid rise to $109.59, this momentum is counterbalanced by a massive near-term catalyst: the coordinated IEA stock release of 400 million barrels (including 172 million from the US SPR) scheduled to begin physical delivery the week of March 16, which acts as a powerful cap on the immediate rally. Given the extreme volatility regime (70.8% annualized vol), the prediction intervals have been widened by 12.5% relative to the baseline using a log-normal distribution to capture the persistent geopolitical risk premium from the Strait of Hormuz closure.

metadata.horizon_rationale: For the 10-day horizon, the point forecast is set at $98.37, aligned with the ARIMA baseline and a flat-trend random-walk approach. Linear trend projections (which suggest a rise to $122.42) are highly unrealistic given the coordinated international supply intervention. However, structural physical supply deficits (~8 million bpd due to the Strait of Hormuz conflict, offset only partially by the IEA/SPR releases and OPEC+ gradual April unwinding of cuts) prevent a significant price collapse. Reflecting the extreme volatility regime, prediction intervals are widened by 12.5%, modeled with a log-normal distribution to represent the skewed potential for price spikes if hostilities escalate.

Although short-term momentum is strongly bullish, a record coordinated 400-million-barrel emergency stock release by the IEA and US SPR (starting the week of March 16) acts as a near-term ceiling. metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 21-day horizon, statistical trend models break down and linear extrapolation ($150.65) is highly speculative. We weight the news context and the flat ARIMA baseline ($97.55) heavily. The massive SPR/IEA release and expected macro demand destruction (estimated at 1 million bpd through April due to travel/refinery cuts) will stabilize prices in the high $90s. The 12.5% interval widening under the extreme volatility regime (70.8% vol) reflects the substantial long-term uncertainty around whether the Strait of Hormuz will remain blocked or if a ceasefire will be brokered, leading to a wider but carefully calibrated set of log-normal quantiles.

metadata.agent_rationale: WTI prices experienced extreme volatility (rolling annualised vol of 71.2%) in March 2026 due to the outbreak of the 2026 Iran War, Strait of Hormuz closure, and a record-breaking 172-million-barrel U.S. SPR release. On Monday, March 23, prices fell 10% to ~$89/b on headlines of potential negotiations. The forecast points are calibrated down to Monday's close to reflect this latest catalyst, and the prediction intervals are widened by 20% using a split-normal methodology based on the ARIMA baseline's implied uncertainty.
metadata.horizon_rationale: Point forecast adjusted to Monday, March 23 market close (~$89.00/b), which dropped 10% on headlines of potential U.S.-Iran negotiations. Prediction intervals are widened by 20% to account for the extreme volatility regime (annualised vol of 71.2%) and binary geopolitical risk.

metadata.horizon_rationale: Point forecast incorporates the slight downward drift from the ARIMA baseline and a domestic supply glut in the U.S. as a massive 172 million barrel Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) emergency release begins. Prediction intervals are widened by 20% due to the extreme volatility regime.

metadata.horizon_rationale: At the 21-day horizon, the point forecast is set to $86.50/b, reflecting a gradual de-escalation of the conflict as diplomatic negotiations proceed and physical SPR supply hits the U.S. Gulf Coast, dampening WTI relative to Brent. Wide intervals cover binary outcomes of successful negotiations vs. renewed escalation.
